---
suppress-bibliography: true
bibliography: references.bib
---
[JS: General comment: Focus less on pathofysiology and more on what is previously know in hetogenity in prediabetes. What is the added angle of incretins and glucagon in conjucntion with glucose metabolism. And what
# Conclusions
[JS: I think you could add some more to this paragraph. I think this should briefly summarize your answer to your aim. So I think adding in short to explain how these clusters were linked to adiposity]

In this study, we used K-means clustering to identify three subgroups with distinct responses in glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon derived from the three-point OGTT. The clustering revealed phenotypes that differed not only in glycemic status, but also in distinct incretin and glucagon regulation.

[JS: I think the text goes strait into cluster 3 and then gets a bit heavy with a lot of pathophysiology. Do not turn every stone in your results, but think about what you want to highlight. Maybe consider focusing more on how the clusters differ/are similiar in their characterization rather than how each physiological response is shown across each cluster.
My suggestions is:
1. To briefly start with what previous metabolic clusters have typically consisted of, and then add the perspective of including incretins and glucagon in group with elevated risk of type 2 diabetes
2. Maybe focus what the added dimension of incretin and glucagon to glucose metabolism]

Cluster 3 was the most metabolically compromised and adipose subgroup, characterized by insulin resistance, compensatory hyperinsulinemia, dysglycemia and the highest VAT, BMI and body fat percentage. This group was also the oldest and had the highest proportion of smokers and individuals with type 2 diabetes, IFG+IGT and IGT. Cluster 3 had elevated fasting insulin (53.7 pmol/L), a 2-hour insulin of 389.2 pmol/L and a 2hPG of 9.1 mmol/L [JS: Keep the results for the results section, unless you are comparing to earlier studies. You have charterized this poulation in the sentence, previously.]. This pattern is consistent with insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia, suggesting preserved beta-cell function, although insulin hypersecretion was insufficient to compensate for the insulin resistance [@defronzo2004]. [JS: I think you could merge a lot of this into the main message that cluster 3 is a group characterized by insulin resistance with preserved beta-cell function, as shown in earlier studies. Also, wait to discuss fat distribution until the next section of the discussion to remove redundant information]

[JS: Now, I would suggest building on how glucagon acts in a cluster with insulin resistance, and then building on how GIP and GLP-1 act in conjunction within this cluster. Then you could do the same on cluster 2 depending on what you want to highlight. ]

Glucagon regulation was also impaired in cluster 3, with limited early glucagon suppression despite adequate late glucagon suppression. Low or negative glucagon suppression may be consistent with continued hepatic glucose output in the early postprandial period, potentially contributing to elevated glucose levels. This is consistent with prior evidence linking impaired early glucagon suppression to insulin resistance and progression to type 2 diabetes [@færch2016; @wagner2017]. Cluster 3 had similar overall glucagon suppression to cluster 1 (45.3% vs 47%)[JS: agian numbers are shown in the results. Delete this to get more characters], despite different early and late patterns. This may reflect delayed and compensatory glucagon suppression in cluster 3, rather than the early suppression in cluster 1 [@gromada2007]. In contrast, cluster 2 had low glucagon suppression at all intervals despite the highest insulin sensitivity and no elevated glucose levels. The finding of low glucagon suppression among insulin-sensitive individuals is in line with previous findings [@færch2016; @wagner2017], which suggests that cluster 2's low glucagon suppression may reflect a metabolically healthier phenotype rather than dysregulation. Clusters 1 and 2 had similar insulin sensitivity and proportions of individuals with NGT, but different incretin secretions, with cluster 1 having the lowest incretin secretion across all clusters. The preserved glucagon suppression in cluster 1, despite low incretin levels, suggests that their insulin secretion was sufficient to inhibit glucagon secretion under current conditions [@gromada2007]. While incretins are typically important regulators of both insulin and glucagon secretion, these mechanisms are known to be glucose-dependent [@christensen2011], indicating that in insulin-sensitive individuals, adequate glucagon suppression can be maintained even when incretin secretion is reduced. However, the observation of lower incretin secretion in cluster 1 also raises the question of whether this reflects a subgroup at higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes, as lower levels of GLP-1 previously have been found to already be present in individuals with prediabetes in the ADDITION-PRO cohort [@færch2015][JS: This sentence is a bit speculative, maybe you could mention this short in prespectives of you findings]. Conversely, cluster 3 exhibited higher incretin secretion than cluster 1, underscoring that the interplay between impaired glucose metabolism, obesity and incretin secretion is complex[JS: This sentence does not say a lot and could be combined with the next sentence that conveys the point]. The higher GLP-1 secretion in cluster 3 may reflect a compensatory mechanism to maintain sufficient insulin secretion as suggested previously [@færch2008]. Elevated GIP may also partly reflect adiposity-related GIP secretion, consistent with prior evidence linking obesity to higher fasting and postprandial GIP levels [@vilsbøll2003]. In summary, these findings highlight that the hormonal responses of dysglycemia differ between subgroups, providing nuance to the heterogeneity among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes [JS: you could save space by deleting this sentence. You are already metioning this in the discussion].

**Body fat distribution and sex differences\**
Beyond hormonal responses, the three clusters differed in their associations with body fat distribution. The insulin-resistant profile of cluster 3 was associated with higher VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage [JS: Higher compared to what?], consistent with previous findings from the ADDITION-PRO cohort [@philipsen2015] and evidence linking VAT to iIFG, iIGT, IFG+IGT and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes [@borel2015] [JS: This sentence is quite long and could be split into two]. Clusters 1 and 2 differed markedly in SAT and VAT/SAT ratio, while cluster 2 had slightly higher VAT[JS: Is this based on your descriptive results or your prespecified analysis? I think you should stick to discussing your analysis results, as I think it would otherwise get confusing] but lower body fat percentage than cluster 1. Higher SAT has previously been suggested to be a protective metabolic trait, as it was found to be inversely associated with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes [@borel2015]. This suggests a more favorable fat distribution in cluster 1, with relatively greater subcutaneous fat despite higher overall adiposity than cluster 2[JS: Maybe wait with this statement until you have covered the sex aspect, as cluster 2 is a group with a higher proportion of women]. 

Sex-stratified clustering produced similar phenotypes in both men and women as the main analysis. Women had higher total GLP-1 and GIP but lower glucagon suppression across all clusters, consistent with previous findings in the same cohort [@færch2015]. Sex-by-cluster interaction terms were non-significant in all models, with the largest difference in VAT and SAT for clusters 1 and 3 and VAT/SAT ratio for cluster 1. However, interaction tests are often underpowered [@sommet2023], and sex-specific associations therefore cannot be ruled out[JS: ]. Fat distribution is known to differ between men and women [@bredella2017], and in particular, VAT has been shown to have a stronger association with cardiometabolic risk in women [@schorr2018]. Overall, consistency between non-stratified and stratified clustering solutions supports the robustness of the primary analysis, consistent with the findings in a previous clustering study of prediabetes [@stroebel2025]. Notably, Stroebel et al. found that a subcutaneous-adipose group with 84% women and the highest proportion of SAT, showed the lowest risk for developing type 2 diabetes despite high BMI and waist circumference, suggesting that sex differences in fat distribution may be clinically relevant[JS: Relevant point (maybe a bit general knowledgee) but could be better if you specified why this are relavent in this study, and include point after you have adressed this].

**Comparison with other phenotyping studies**\
To our knowledge, this is the first study to include incretin and glucagon responses in a cluster analysis among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes. However, the identified subgroups broadly align with previous evidence on heterogeneity among individuals with prediabetes or at elevated risk of type 2 diabetes [@stroebel2025; @wagner2021]. Cluster 3 corresponded to the overweight, insulin-resistant group with a high proportion of individuals with type 2 diabetes, and clusters 1 and 2 corresponded to the more insulin-sensitive subgroups with mostly normoglycemic individuals. Unlike previous studies using follow-up data to assess risk of type 2 diabetes [@stroebel2025; @wagner2021], the present cross-sectional design could not assess temporal associations. Wagner et al. [@wagner2021] identified three high-risk clusters characterized by visceral adiposity and insulin resistance that broadly corresponded to cluster 3, while their three low-risk clusters corresponded to clusters 1 and 2. Interestingly, Stroebel et al. [@stroebel2025] found a subgroup with a lower VAT/SAT ratio and lower risk of type 2 diabetes despite higher BMI and waist circumference, consistent with our findings that cluster 1 had a lower VAT/SAT ratio despite higher overall adiposity than cluster 2. While differences in variable selection and study design limit direct comparisons with prior studies, the overall cluster pattern was consistent across studies. However, the inclusion of incretin and glucagon responses appeared to reveal heterogeneity beyond glycemic and obesity measures. Notably, cluster 3 exhibited higher incretin secretion than cluster 1, despite more impaired glucose regulation and higher adiposity. Moreover, Cluster 2 had the lowest glucagon suppression but highest incretin secretion, suggesting that the primary distinction between clusters 1 and 2 lies in their incretin and glucagon responses.

[JS: This paragraph could be shorten to one sentence or deleted. It is a very valid point (could be included in the appendix to the article?), but seems a bit out of the scope of this study. As I see it if you want to know something about clustered phenotypes unsupervised learning is the way. But if you want to make precise decision like type of drug the John Dennis approach is the way to go. I would suggest to focus on limitation on hard clustering as the]
While the identification of metabolic subgroups reflects a broader shift in diabetes [@ahlqvist2018; @tobias2023] and prediabetes research [@wagner2021; @stroebel2025], the clinical use of data-driven phenotyping approaches has been challenged. Dennis et al. [@dennis2019] found that simple, continuous features, such as age at diagnosis, predicted glycemic progression as well as clustering-based approaches. Furthermore, hard clustering methods like K-means clustering raise questions about how well observations fit their assigned cluster, and clustering should therefore be accompanied by measures of classification uncertainty [@mori2025]. Consequently, an international consensus report by the Precision Medicine in Diabetes Initiative has noted that simple, continuous features remain the most promising approach for predicting treatment outcomes [@tobias2023]. Rather than aiming to predict outcomes, clustering methods may therefore be most useful as an exploratory tool for assessing pathophysiological heterogeneity that standard glycemic measures cannot capture.

# Strengths and limitations

The strengths of this study include the relatively large cohort of individuals with NGT, prediabetes and type 2 diabetes, sensitive and specific methods of measuring glucagon, GLP-1 and GIP through the OGTT [@holst1983; @orskov1994; @toft-nielsen2001], and detailed assessment of fat distribution by ultrasound. However, several limitations should be considered. First, the cross-sectional design precludes causal and temporal interpretations between cluster characteristics and fat distribution. Second, we did not perform fully sex-stratified clustering and regression analyses, which may have limited identification of sex-specific phenotypes. However, such analyses would also have reduced sample size and statistical power. Given known sex differences in fat distribution and incretin responses, future studies with larger samples should consider sex-stratified clustering as the primary approach. Moreover, because male sex contributed to the ADDITION-PRO risk score [@glümer2004], women in the cohort may have had a higher burden of other risk factors, potentially affecting comparability between sexes. Third, extreme values may have disproportionately influenced cluster centers, and the silhouette width may indicate overlapping phenotypes for some individuals [@mori2025]. Fourth, excluded participants tended to have higher BMI, visceral fat and HbA1c, suggesting that the included population may represent a metabolically healthier high-risk subgroup, which may have affected both the generalisability of the identified clusters and their associations with fat distribution. Finally, the clinical relevance of hormonal differences between subgroups remains unclear, and future prospective studies should assess whether these relate to progression to type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular complications or responses to prevention strategies.

In summary, we identified three distinct subgroups among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes, characterized by differences in incretin secretion, glucagon regulation and body fat distribution. Subgroups with similar glycemic status differed markedly in incretin secretion and glucagon regulation, suggesting that these dimensions capture metabolic heterogeneity that standard glycemic classification does not reflect. These findings may have implications for more targeted prevention and treatment strategies [JS: You have not discussed how these results can define such actions. I know character count is the challenge, but I could use some perspectives on these results. For example, in prediabetes the challenge is that some individuals develop type 2 diabetes, some regress to below the threshold of prediabetes, and some remain in prediabetes. All states have an impact on long-term cardiometabolic health. One idea would be whether incretin and glucagon compensation have any influence on this. The long-term goal as I see]. Prospective studies in other cohorts should examine whether the identified subgroups can be replicated and whether they differ in their risk of developing type 2 diabetes and their responses to prevention and treatment strategies.
